days.content:
A foot of new snow and strong southwesterly winds formed drifted slabs on steep, leeward slopes and in rocky couloirs. You could trigger an avalanche where you find a deeper pocket of stiff snow that cracks underfoot. Be on the lookout for these drifts below ridgelines on leeward slopes and in steep, rocky, cross-loaded couloirs. The most dangerous slopes face north, northeast, and east. Most of these avalanches will be small, but even a small slide can ruin your day if it carries you into a terrain trap, like rocks or a cliff.

Sunny skies increase the chance for a natural wet avalanche cycle in the afternoon. Look for rollerballs as an early warning sign to head for the shade.

Images.caption: It sure is nice to see fresh snow, especially at the very end of April! Where was this all winter... The most recent storm dropped 6-12" of wet heavy snow across the high country. This new snow will settle in the coming days and could potentially shed off sunny slopes later in the day under sunny skies.
Images.altText: A ruler measuring new snow.

Images.caption: New snow and wind will once again increase the avalanche danger in the alpine. Watch out for fresh drifts and pockets of stiffer snow in the alpine. Even a small avalanche could be dangerous if it wipes you off your feet in extreme terrain.
Images.altText: A snowboarder cuts across a steep slope with a fracture line of an avalanche above them. They are in the middle of a crumbling wind slab avalanche

Images.caption: This avalanche was small, but it did manage to knock a backcountry traveller off their feet, and it was triggered in an area of firm, wind-drifted snow near the top of the slope. Assess the reactivity of these stiffened slabs by looking for cracking in drifted features. It's also a good idea to look below to assess the consequences of taking a small ride before committing to a steep slope.
Images.altText: A backcountry rider stands in a ravine with rocks to their left, snow to their right, and an avalanche debris pile above them.

days.content: You can trigger an avalanche where you find more than about 10 inches of new or drifted snow. High-elevation slopes that held snow before this storm are the most likely to be loaded and will be the most dangerous. You can reduce your chances of triggering an avalanche by avoiding rounded, firm pillows of snow near ridgelines and along gully walls.

Images.caption: If you find stiff areas of drifted snow you could trigger a small avalanche. These avalanches could be dangerous if you get knocked off your feet and tumble down the slope, especially in consequential terrain.
Images.altText: A small avalanche in steep terrain.

Images.caption: A small avalanche triggered in the wrong place can quickly turn a good day into a bad one. Luckily for this rider, they avoided getting carried into the rocks. This avalanche was small, but it did manage to knock a backcountry traveler off their feet, and it was triggered in an area of firm, wind-drifted snow near the top of the slope. Assess the reactivity of these stiffened slabs by looking for cracking in drifted features. It's also a good idea to look below to assess the consequences of taking a small ride before committing to a steep slope.
Images.altText: A skier looking up at a freshly triggered avalanche in a gully.

Images.caption: If riding in steeper terrain, manage your sluff so you don't get knocked down or swept into consequential terrain features. Any avalanche will likely be small, but even a small avalanche can quickly turn a good day into a bad one if triggered in extreme terrain.
Images.altText: Small sluff in steep gully

days.content:
Most terrain in this area is safe from avalanches due to the lack of snow. New snow is not enough to increase the avalanche danger, but winds could form small pockets of drifted snow below ridgelines in the alpine.

Look for and avoid these drifted pockets around rocky outcrops and in cross-loaded couloirs. Even a small avalanche can take you for a nasty ride in extreme terrain.

Images.caption: While conditions are generally safe, even a small avalanche triggered in the wrong place can quickly turn a good day into a bad one. Luckily for this rider, they avoided getting carried into the rocks. This Wind Slab avalanche was triggered near Guanella Pass on April 18th. This avalanche was small, but it did manage to knock a backcountry traveller off their feet, and it was triggered in an area of firm, wind-drifted snow near the top of the slope. Assess the reactivity of these stiffened slabs by looking for cracking in drifted features. It's also a good idea to look below to assess the consequences of taking a small ride before committing to a steep slope.
Images.altText: A skier looking up at an avalanche they triggered.

Images.caption: It's LOW (1 of 5) danger and conditions are generally safe on most slopes. Still, as backcountry travelers step into bigger and more consequential terrain, you might be able to find stiff areas of drifted snow where you could trigger a small avalanche. These avalanches could be dangerous if you get knocked off your feet and tumble down the slope, especially in consequential terrain.
Images.altText: A snowboarder triggering an avalanche in a steep couloir.

days.content: Approach steep alpine terrain along the leeward sides of ridgelines with caution. You can trigger a small avalanche that could grow large enough to knock you off your feet and take you for a dangerous ride where you encounter stiff, wind-drifted snow. Consider the consequences of being caught in a small avalanche before you travel on slopes steeper than around 35 degrees where you encounter more than about a foot of new snow.

Images.caption: Conditions are generally safe on most slopes. Still, as backcountry travelers step into bigger and more consequential terrain, you might be able to find stiff areas of drifted snow where you could trigger a small avalanche. These avalanches could be dangerous if you get knocked off your feet and tumble down the slope, especially in consequential terrain.
Images.altText: A small avalanche in steep terrain.

Images.caption: A small avalanche triggered in the wrong place can quickly turn a good day into a bad one. Luckily for this rider, they avoided getting carried into the rocks. This Wind Slab avalanche was triggered near Guanella Pass on Saturday. This avalanche was small, but it did manage to knock a backcountry traveller off their feet, and it was triggered in an area of firm, wind-drifted snow near the top of the slope. Assess the reactivity of these stiffened slabs by looking for cracking in drifted features. It's also a good idea to look below to assess the consequences of taking a small ride before committing to a steep slope.
Images.altText: A skier looking up at a freshly triggered avalanche in a gully.

Images.caption: If riding in steeper terrain, manage your sluff so you don't get knocked down or swept into consequential terrain features. Any avalanche will likely be small, but even a small avalanche can quickly turn a good day into a bad one if triggered in extreme terrain.
Images.altText: Small sluff in steep gulley

days.content: Over a foot of snow fell over the last couple of days with moderate southwest winds drifting additional snow onto leeward slopes. Most avalanches you trigger will be small, but if you find a wind-drifted slope just below the ridgeline, you might trigger an avalanche big enough to bury you on a steep, sustained slope. If you are planning on traveling on slopes much steeper than around 35 degrees, then pay attention to terrain traps such as a cliff below you, and consider how large the slope is and if it could produce a large avalanche before traveling directly on it.

Images.caption: Sunny, warm weather this time of year means there's potential for small, loose activity on steep, sunny slopes in the afternoon. Head for the shade or less-steep slopes if you find yourself sinking above your ankles into wet, mushy snow.
Images.altText: A ski track near a small loose avalanche below rocks

Images.caption: It's LOW (1 of 5) danger and conditions are generally safe on most slopes. Still, as backcountry travelers step into bigger and more consequential terrain, you might be able to find stiff areas of drifted snow where you could trigger a small avalanche. These avalanches could be dangerous if you get knocked off your feet and tumble down the slope, especially in consequential terrain.
Images.altText: A small avalanche in steep terrain.

This Wind Slab avalanche was triggered near Guanella Pass on Saturday. This avalanche was small, but it did manage to knock a backcountry traveller off their feet, and it was triggered in an area of firm, wind-drifted snow near the top of the slope. Assess the reactivity of these stiffened slabs by looking for cracking in drifted features. It's also a good idea to look below to assess the consequences of taking a small ride before committing to a steep slope.
Images.altText: A skier looking up at a freshly triggered avalanche in a gully.

Images.altText: Small sluff in steep gulley

days.content: Generally safe avalanche conditions exist. If you plan on traveling in very steep, consequential alpine terrain, identify and avoid slopes that have more than about 8 inches of wind-drifted snow.

Images.caption: Sunny, warm weather this time of year means there's potential for small, loose activity on steep, sunny slopes in the afternoon. Head for the shade or less-steep slopes if you find yourself sinking above your ankles into wet, mushy snow.
Images.altText: A ski track near a small loose avalanche below rocks

Images.caption: If riding in steeper terrain, manage your sluff so you don't get knocked down or swept into consequential terrain features. Any avalanche will likely be small, but even a small avalanche can quickly turn a good day into a bad one if triggered in extreme terrain.
Images.altText: Small sluff in steep gulley

days.content: Avoid rounded pillows of firm drifted snow in high-elevation terrain where you could trigger an avalanche. These will be found close to ridges and along gully walls. These could knock you off your feet or get large enough to bury you on larger slopes.

Images.caption: If you find stiff areas of drifted snow you could trigger a small avalanche. These avalanches could be dangerous if you get knocked off your feet and tumble down the slope, especially in consequential terrain.
Images.altText: A small avalanche in steep terrain.

Images.caption: A small avalanche triggered in the wrong place can quickly turn a good day into a bad one. Luckily for this rider, they avoided getting carried into the rocks. This avalanche was small, but it did manage to knock a backcountry traveller off their feet, and it was triggered in an area of firm, wind-drifted snow near the top of the slope. Assess the reactivity of these stiffened slabs by looking for cracking in drifted features. It's also a good idea to look below to assess the consequences of taking a small ride before committing to a steep slope.
Images.altText: A skier looking up at a freshly triggered avalanche in a gully.

Images.caption: If riding in steeper terrain, manage your sluff so you don't get knocked down or swept into consequential terrain features. Any avalanche will likely be small, but even a small avalanche can quickly turn a good day into a bad one if triggered in extreme terrain.
Images.altText: Small sluff in steep gulley

days.content: Avoid rounded pillows of firm drifted snow in high-elevation terrain where you could trigger an avalanche. These will be found close to ridges and along gully walls. The avalanche you trigger will most likely be harmless unless you are in consequential terrain.

Images.caption: If you find stiff areas of drifted snow you could trigger a small avalanche. These avalanches could be dangerous if you get knocked off your feet and tumble down the slope, especially in consequential terrain.
Images.altText: A small avalanche in steep terrain.

It's also a good idea to look below to assess the consequences of taking a small ride before committing to a steep slope.
Images.altText: A skier looking up at a freshly triggered avalanche in a gully.

Images.altText: Small sluff in steep gulley

days.content: Most terrain in this area is safe from avalanches due to the lack of snow. Any remaining snowpack will continue to melt and is unlikely to avalanche.

Images.caption: This year's snowpack (the black line) has been historically shallow, setting new record lows for average snow water equivalent. As such, many lower-elevation areas are already melted out, or the snowpack is patchy and discontinuous. This makes avalanches unlikely.
Images.altText: The snow water equivalent graph for the San Juan river basins.
